data.title: Comstock announces sale of northern mining targets to Mackay Precious Metals
data.description:
• Comstock (NYSE:LODE) has agreed to sell 100% of its northernmost mining claims, exploration rights, and town lots currently owned by Comstock’s wholly-owned subsidiary Comstock Northern Exploration, along with its 25% stake in Pelen, to Mackay Precious Metals for $2.75M.
  • The deal also includes a 1.5% NSR production royalty on silver, gold, and other minerals extracted from these properties, in perpetuity.
  • The company received $1.3M in cash, with another $0.45M due by February 15, 2025. An additional $1M will be paid within 45 days of Mackay’s public listing, either in cash or registered shares, with the $1M value guaranteed by October 31, 2025.
  • On June 30, 2023, Comstock signed a lease agreement with Mackay, receiving $3.2M in initial and ongoing payments.
  • An additional $0.5M will be paid by February 15, 2025, bringing total lease payments to $3.7M, separate from the $2.75M sale price; the lease was terminated on December 18, 2024.
  • The Northern Targets include the Gold Hill and Occidental Lode claims in Storey County, Nevada, while Pelen owns certain claims adjacent to and/or relevant to these northern claim groups.
  • LODE shares were up over 2.5% on Friday.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Nasdaq, S&P, Dow trade advance as core PCE comes in softer than expected
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages trade higher on Friday, after the November's core PCE report came in cooler than expected.

The S&P 500 (SP500) was +0.7%, the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was +0.7% and the Dow (DJI) was +0.8%.

The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) fell 4 basis points to 4.52%. The 2-year yield (US2Y) fell 3 basis points to 4.29%.

The Federal Reserve's favored inflation gauge - core PCE - ticked down in November, and edged up 0.1% M/M in November, less than the +0.2% consensus.

A stopgap spending plan backed by President-elect Donald Trump was rejected in the House of Representatives, further fueling concerns of a government shutdown.

"The big question now is whether a U.S. government shutdown is about to happen," Deutsche Bank's Jim Reid said.

The latest bill was voted down by 235 votes to 174. The funding is set to expire on Friday night.

"A short-lived shutdown affects government workers, but has limited economic impact. The longer a shutdown lasts, the more disruptive it is to the U.S. economy. Economic data may not get published in a shutdown," UBS' Paul Donovan said.

Wall Street's bounce back on Thursday following its worst session in over three months ran out of steam in the final hour of trading.

"For those wanting a quiet run up to Xmas, the good news is that there hasn't been any real follow-through to the Fed-induced slump on Wednesday. The bad news is that an initial recovery in markets struggled to gain traction yesterday," Reid added.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Carnival in charts: Passengers carried reaches 3.0M in Q4; occupancy at 103%
Why is Novo Nordisk crashing after data for next-gen weight loss drug?
data.description:
Novo Nordisk (NVO) plunged to a 52-week low on Friday after the Danish drugmaker released Phase 3 results for its next-gen weight loss therapy, CagriSema, as data gave a pause for its investors, betting on an increasingly competitive market.

While Novo's (NVO) REDEFINE 1 Phase 3 trial reached the main goal for CagriSema against the placebo, the once-weekly injectable caused only up to ~20% weight loss over 68 weeks in the intent-to-treat population.

Shares of its rival weight loss drug developers Eli Lilly (LLY) and Viking Therapeutics (VKTX) climbed after the readout, as analysts questioned NVO's future role in a market expected to reach $150B by 2030, according to some estimates.

According to UBS, the reading not only fell short of the company's prior guidance of 25% but also ~23% of weight loss that Eli Lilly (LLY) has recorded for tirzepatide, its FDA-approved weight loss therapy marketed as Zepbound.

"We view today's CagriSema's P3 obesity readout a positive read-through to LLY, validating again tirzepatide's best-in-class profile," UBS analyst Trung Huynh argued, noting that the Indiana-based drugmaker has its next-gen weight loss therapy retratude as a best-in-class asset in development.

RBC Capital Markets agreed, noting in a research piece: "While results are statistically significant and certainly meaningful for patients, they likely fall well below the expectations of investors, many of whom were anticipating headline weight loss closer to 25%."

"Due to underperformance of efficacy expectations, anticipate NVO shares opening down and LLY up meaningfully," analyst Evan David Seigerman wrote with Outperform recommendations on NVO and LLY.

Additionally, pointing out that CagriSema's efficacy now appears mostly in line with Amgen's (AMGN) weight loss candidate, MariTide, UBS' Huynh cites positive read-through and a competitive advantage to the U.S. drugmaker, given its once-monthly regimen. UBS has Neutral ratings on NVO and AMGN with a Buy rating on LLY.

However, for others, including Seeking Alpha Investing Group Leader Danil Sereda, NVO's selloff has uncovered a buying opportunity.

While CagriSema data might have triggered concerns over the company's ability to fight competition, "based on Novo Nordisk's strong financials, robust pipeline, and market dominance, I think those risks seem manageable," Sereda wrote.

"The almost 20% dip seems to be a rare buying opportunity for long-term buyers, in my view," he added.

data.title: GSK reports mixed results from Phase 3 ovarian cancer study
data.description:
GSK (NYSE:GSK) reported mixed results from a Phase 3 study evaluating Zejula, or niraparib, and Jemperli, or dostarlimab, in the treatment of advanced ovarian cancer.

The U.K. drugmaker said that while the study met its primary endpoint of progression-free survival, or PFS, it failed to meet the key secondary endpoint of overall survival.

The study met the primary endpoint of PFS by demonstrating a statistically significant difference with the addition of dostarlimab to both standard of care carboplatin-paclitaxel chemotherapy and niraparib maintenance, with or without bevacizumab, according to a statement.

GSK plans to present the data at an upcoming scientific meeting.

data.title: Carbon Done Right announces non-brokered private placement
data.description:
• Carbon Done Right Developments (TSXV:KLX:CA) on Friday announced its intention to carry out a non-brokered private placement to raise gross proceeds of up to $250,000 consisting of units offered at $0.015 per unit.
  • The company plans to utilize offering proceeds for continued investment into operations and corporate support for the company’s flagship forest carbon restoration project and in order to meet investment requirements associated with the final milestone payment under the previously announced pre-purchase agreement.
  • The offering is expected to close on or around December 23, 2024.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Quantum Biopharma announces purchase of $1M of bitcoin and other cryptocurrencies
data.description:
• Quantum BioPharma (NASDAQ:QNTM) has purchased $1M bitcoin and other cryptocurrencies as part of its strategic efforts.
  • Going forward, the company will allow for future financing and other transactions to be carried out in cryptocurrency.
  • The company is now set up to receive financing in cryptocurrencies as well as executing other types of transactions in cryptocurrencies.
  • The company is holding all its cryptocurrency with a fully compliant custodian. 
  • QNTM shares were down over 17% on Friday.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Applied Digital initiated at Overweight due to HPC data center growth: CF
data.description:
Applied Digital (NASDAQ:APLD) was initiated with an Overweight rating by Cantor Fitzgerald as the financial firm believes the company will become a large high-performance computing data center operator in the U.S.

The firm also assigned the Dallas-based digital infrastructure and cloud service provider, formerly known as Applied Blockchain, a $15 price target. The stock value has increased 33% over the past six months, but suffered a setback on Thursday when it closed down 8%. It was relatively flat during Friday market action.

Applied Digital is constructing an HPC data center in Ellendale, N.D. Earlier this week it announced the successful energization of its on-site main substation transformer at the site.

"This achievement will allow the Company to initiate equipment commissioning activities, bringing us closer to delivering the infrastructure we believe will define the future of AI and digital transformation in Ellendale and beyond," said Todd Gale, Chief Development Officer at Applied Digital.

"APLD is taking the 'if you build it, they will come' approach, as negotiations with 'Hyperscaler 1' have continuously been extended," said Cantor Fitzgerald analyst Brett Knoblauch, in a Fridat investor note.

Knoblauch said there is an 80% chance the first lease will be signed before the end of January. However, if "Hyperscale 1" ultimately declines the deal, it will take two to three more months before the lease is signed by a different hyperscaler.

In late November, Applied Digital entered into a promissory note with Macquarie Equipment Capital for a loan of $150M.

data.title: Consumer inflation expectations tick down in second December print: UMich
data.description: The University of Michigan's U.S. Consumer Sentiment Index was unchanged at 74.0 in December's second reading, up from 71.8 in November, as inflation expectations slipped further.

data.title: As Trump aims to slash federal spending, Wells Fargo economists see no major cuts in 2025
data.description:
President-elect Donald Trump upon returning to Washington will seek to jettison what he sees as excessive federal spending, and Wells Fargo economists project there will be marginal reductions next year with minimal impact on U.S. economic activity.

But tackling fraud targeted at the government may be worth some effort, they said.

Trump will step into the White House next month seeing that the government had spent $6.8T in fiscal year 2024 that ended in September. Wells Fargo Economics broadly broke down the figure to ~$4.1T in mandatory, or entitlement, spending, and $1.8T in discretionary spending. The latter undergoes an annual appropriations process, and Social Security and Medicaid fall under mandatory spending.

“We think some reductions in federal spending and employment on the margin are plausible over the next couple of years,” Wells Fargo Chief Economist Jay Bryson said, “but probably not on the scale that they will have large implications for a U.S. economy that has almost 160 million nonfarm employees and nearly $30 trillion of annual GDP.”

Overall, “we are assuming relative status quo for these areas in 2025, not major changes,” Bryson said in a research report published this week. Bryson, with Wells Fargo Senior Economist Michael Pugliese, noted it would take an act of Congress to materially increase or decrease federal spending.

Spending on Social Security, Medicare, Medicaid, national defense and debt-service costs came in at ~$4.7T, nearly equal to the $4.9T in revenues the government had gathered. “It would take the elimination of all other spending, from veterans benefits to border security to education spending, to balance the federal budget if [other] spending categories are left unchanged,” Bryson said.

Wells Fargo said it’s “skeptical” about major-scale reductions in mandatory spending “given the enduring popularity” of many federal programs, chiefly Social Security and Medicare, the two largest.

Within non-defense discretionary spending, chopping down government headcount is unlikely to produce meaningful budgetary savings, they said. That’s partially because the $384B in estimated pay and benefits of civilian employees makes up less than 6% of federal spending.

But budget cuts are not impossible, Bryson said, highlighting that getting rid of fraud targeting the government could save hundreds of billions of dollars each year. The non-partisan Government Accountability Office in April said the government lost $233B-$521B annually between FY 2018-2022 because of fraud.

“Combating fraud is a challenging undertaking, but even if done successfully, reducing fraudulent payments to zero is unlikely to be enough to single-handedly put the federal government on a fiscally sustainable path,” Bryson said.

The U.S. budget deficit hit a record high $1.8T in FY24, with the government paying for it through the sale of Treasury bonds (US10Y) and other securities, stoking worries about bond oversupply. Trump’s new advisory group, Department of Government Efficiency, whose co-head is Elon Musk, is looking for overall federal cuts of at least $2T.

JPMorgan analysts this month said they expect aerospace and defense companies such as Lockheed Martin (LMT), RTX (RTX), and General Dynamics (GD) to face greater risk of federal budget cuts in 2025.
